annotations.label: Accuracy and precision

anchor.snippet: accuracy is how close a given set of measurements is to the true value and precision is how close the measurements are to each other

annotations.note: Informal metrological concept; Mathlib has no definitions for empirical accuracy or precision of measurements.

annotations.label: Trueness (ISO)

anchor.snippet: the closeness of agreement between the arithmetic mean of a large number of test results and the true or accepted reference value

annotations.note: ISO-style metrological notion of trueness is not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Accurate vs precise sample

anchor.snippet: the sample or set can be said to be accurate if their average is close to the true value of the quantity being measured, while the set can be said to be precise if their standard deviation is relatively small

annotations.note: Mathlib defines sample mean and standard deviation but does not formalize 'accurate' or 'precise' sample qualifiers.

annotations.label: Accuracy of a measurement system

anchor.snippet: the accuracy of a measurement system is the degree of closeness of measurements of a quantity to that quantity's true value

annotations.note: No formalization of 'measurement system' or its accuracy in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Precision of a measurement system

anchor.snippet: The precision of a measurement system, related to reproducibility and repeatability , is the degree to which repeated measurements under unchanged conditions show the same results

annotations.note: Metrological precision is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Measurement resolution

anchor.snippet: measurements may also have a measurement resolution , which is the smallest change in the underlying physical quantity that produces a response in the measurement

annotations.note: Physical measurement resolution is not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Accuracy and precision in numerical analysis

anchor.snippet: In numerical analysis , accuracy is also the nearness of a calculation to the true value; while precision is the resolution of the representation

annotations.note: Mathlib does not formalize numerical-analysis notions of accuracy or representation precision.

annotations.label: Accuracy of fire (military)

anchor.snippet: accuracy refers primarily to the accuracy of fire ( justesse de tir ), the precision of fire expressed by the closeness of a grouping of shots at and around the centre of the target

annotations.note: Military ballistic terminology has no analogue in Mathlib.

annotations.label: ISO 5725-1 accuracy, trueness, and precision

anchor.snippet: trueness is the closeness of the mean of a set of measurement results to the actual (true) value, that is the systematic error, and precision is the closeness of agreement among a set of results, that is the random error

annotations.note: ISO 5725 metrology definitions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Accuracy in industrial instrumentation

anchor.snippet: accuracy is the measurement tolerance, or transmission of the instrument and defines the limits of the errors made when the instrument is used in normal operating conditions

annotations.note: Industrial instrumentation tolerance is not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Standard error of the average

anchor.snippet: the precision of the average is equal to the known standard deviation of the process divided by the square root of the number of measurements averaged

annotations.note: Mathlib proves variance of a sum of independent variables equals the sum of variances (`IndepFun.variance_sum`), from which the σ/√n formula for the sample mean follows, but the formula for the standard error of the mean itself is not stated as a named lemma.

annotations.label: Central limit theorem for averaged measurements

anchor.snippet: the central limit theorem shows that the probability distribution of the averaged measurements will be closer to a normal distribution than that of individual measurements

annotations.note: The 1-dimensional CLT for i.i.d. variables with mean μ and variance v converging to a Gaussian is formalized as `tendstoInDistribution_inv_sqrt_mul_sum_sub`.

annotations.label: Bias

anchor.snippet: the difference between the mean of the measurements and the reference value, the bias

annotations.note: Statistical bias of an estimator is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Significant figures convention

anchor.snippet: Where not explicitly stated, the margin of error is understood to be one-half the value of the last significant place

annotations.note: Significant-figures notation is a presentational convention not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Margin of error from significant figures

anchor.snippet: a recording of 843.6 m, or 843.0 m, or 800.0 m would imply a margin of 0.05 m

annotations.note: Worked example of a notational convention; not a mathematical statement to formalize.

annotations.label: Scientific notation precision

anchor.snippet: 8.0 × 10 3 m indicates that the first zero is significant (hence a margin of 50 m) while 8.000 × 10 3 m indicates that all three zeros are significant, giving a margin of 0.5 m

annotations.note: Scientific-notation precision convention is not a Mathlib concept.

annotations.label: Parenthetical uncertainty notation

anchor.snippet: one can use a notation such as 7.54398(23) × 10 −10 m, meaning a range of between 7.54375 and 7.54421 × 10 −10 m

annotations.note: Concise-form uncertainty notation is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Repeatability and reproducibility

anchor.snippet: repeatability — the variation arising when all efforts are made to keep conditions constant by using the same instrument and operator, and repeating during a short time period

annotations.note: Metrological repeatability/reproducibility are not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Engineering precision (3-sigma)

anchor.snippet: precision is often taken as three times Standard Deviation of measurements taken, representing the range that 99.73% of measurements can occur within

annotations.note: Three-sigma engineering convention is not a Mathlib definition; Mathlib has standard deviation but not this convention.

annotations.label: Ergonomist body measurements

anchor.snippet: an ergonomist measuring the human body can be confident that 99.73% of their extracted measurements fall within ± 0.7 cm

annotations.note: Domain-specific application example with no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Binary classification accuracy

anchor.snippet: the accuracy is the proportion of correct predictions (both true positives and true negatives ) among the total number of cases examined

annotations.note: ML classification accuracy is not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Multiclass classification accuracy

anchor.snippet: When computing accuracy in multiclass classification, accuracy is simply the fraction of correct classifications

annotations.note: Multiclass accuracy is an ML notion absent from Mathlib.

annotations.label: 90% accuracy example

anchor.snippet: if a classifier makes ten predictions and nine of them are correct, the accuracy is 90%

annotations.note: Arithmetic illustration of classifier accuracy; no Mathlib counterpart.

annotations.label: Top-5 accuracy

anchor.snippet: a classification is considered correct if the correct classification falls anywhere within the top 5 predictions made by the network

annotations.note: Top-k accuracy is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Accuracy and precision in psychometrics

anchor.snippet: the term accuracy is interchangeably used with validity and constant error . Precision is a synonym for reliability and variable error

annotations.note: Psychometric validity/reliability terminology has no Mathlib analogue.

annotations.label: Precision vs accuracy in logic simulation

anchor.snippet: Precision is measured with respect to detail and accuracy is measured with respect to reality

annotations.note: Logic-simulation terminology is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Precision (information retrieval)

anchor.snippet: precision is defined as the fraction of documents correctly retrieved compared to the documents retrieved (true positives divided by true positives plus false positives)

annotations.note: Information-retrieval precision is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Recall

anchor.snippet: Recall is defined as the fraction of documents correctly retrieved compared to the relevant documents (true positives divided by true positives plus false negatives)

annotations.note: Information-retrieval recall has no Mathlib formalization.

annotations.label: Accuracy (information retrieval)

anchor.snippet: the metric of accuracy is used, is defined as the fraction of documents correctly classified compared to the documents

annotations.note: IR accuracy metric is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Precision at k

anchor.snippet: The measure precision at k , for example, is a measure of precision looking only at the top ten (k=10) search results

annotations.note: Precision-at-k metric is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cognitive accuracy and cognitive precision

anchor.snippet: Cognitive accuracy (C A ) is the propensity of a cognitive process to produce the intended or desired output. Cognitive precision (C P ) is the propensity of a cognitive process to produce the same output

annotations.note: Cognitive-systems terminology has no Mathlib counterpart.
